items.headline: Biotech in New Jersey, seven companies leading the charge
items.preview: New Jersey's biotech landscape features seven innovative companies tackling various health challenges.
items.article:
New Jersey has long been a core hub of the U.S. pharmaceutical industry, home to biotech companies such as Johnson & Johnson and Merck. This article highlights seven biotech companies in New Jersey, showcasing their unique contributions to the industry.

Why this matters: These companies illustrate the innovative spirit of New Jersey's biotech ecosystem, which, while less visible than other hubs like Boston, is making significant strides in areas such as antibiotic discovery, inflammatory disease therapies, and regenerative medicine.

The companies include ArrePath, BioAegis Therapeutics, Celularity, Enalare Therapeutics, EpiBone, Inaedis, and Rocket Pharmaceuticals, each with distinct focuses ranging from AI-driven antibiotic discovery to gene therapies for rare diseases.

items.headline: Dr. Roxana Mehran Appointed President of the American College of Cardiology
items.preview: Dr. Roxana Mehran begins her term as president of the ACC, focusing on cardiovascular care innovation.
items.article:
Dr. Roxana Mehran has been appointed as the new president of the American College of Cardiology (ACC), leading an organization dedicated to cardiovascular medicine. Her presidency aims to enhance heart health globally and integrate scientific innovation into clinical practice.

Why this matters: Dr. Mehran's leadership is expected to drive global partnerships and member engagement, addressing disparities in cardiovascular health and advancing research in the field. Her extensive background in interventional cardiology positions her to influence the future of cardiovascular care significantly.

items.headline: RFK Jr.’s peptide deregulation threatens the foundations of drug safety
items.preview: Concerns arise over RFK Jr.'s push to deregulate peptides, risking drug safety standards.
items.article:
HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced plans to lift federal restrictions on 14 injectable peptides, which has raised alarms regarding drug safety. These peptides, often marketed without proven efficacy, could undermine the drug approval system if allowed to reach consumers without clinical trial data.

Why this matters: The deregulation could legitimize unproven injectables, potentially endangering public health by bypassing established safety protocols. The implications of this move could lead to a larger market for unregulated products, further complicating the landscape of drug safety.

items.headline: Mirror Fragments Block Protein Linked to Alzheimer’s Disease
items.preview: Kobe University researchers develop a novel approach to target amyloid-beta aggregation in Alzheimer's.
items.article:
Researchers at Kobe University have created a molecular interceptor that targets amyloid-beta aggregation, a key process in Alzheimer's disease. By utilizing the principle of molecular chirality, they designed small fragments that can bind to and prevent the aggregation of these disordered proteins.

Why this matters: This innovative approach could lead to new therapeutic strategies for Alzheimer’s and other neurodegenerative diseases, transforming previously 'undruggable' proteins into viable drug targets and potentially improving patient outcomes.

items.headline: Structures of the human glucose-6-phosphate transporter provide insights into its transport cycle and substrate recognition
items.preview: New cryo-EM structures reveal insights into the glucose-6-phosphate transporter and its role in metabolism.
items.article:
The human glucose-6-phosphate transporter (G6PT) has been structurally characterized using cryo-electron microscopy, revealing its role in glucose metabolism and implications for glycogen storage disease type Ib. The study provides insights into the transport cycle and substrate recognition mechanisms of this essential protein.

Why this matters: Understanding the structure and function of G6PT is crucial for addressing metabolic disorders, offering a foundation for future therapeutic developments aimed at improving glucose homeostasis and treating related diseases.
